speaker: interviewer
text: '96. Where did you go after Poland and Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- I lived with my sister in Budapest for eight months. I learned Hungarian. I wanted to go to school. There was no chance to go to school in Budapest because, first of all, I didn't need Hungarian language. I needed to learn English. My aim was to go to America, to my auntie who lived in America. And I was hoping she would sponsor. And everybody wanted to go to America. The lot of us wanted to go to America. And I was so indebted, because they have liberated me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after about eight months, my sister had a baby and my mother couldn't come with me. She helped my sister bring the first-- her first grandson. I went via [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] green border illegally to Austria. In Austria, I went to Vienna-- well, Aus-- Vienna is Austria. And from [? Vin-- ?] Vienna-- I was sent to Enns displaced person camp. How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years. At least two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened at the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In DP camp I became a nurse. I volunteered to work in the hospital, because I felt my mother did that so I have to do that, too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were a lot of transport arriving from east. Lots of Russian people-- Russian Jews, Polish Jews-- who were during the war in Russia. And also, somehow, we had contact-- not somehow, but it was very well arranged-- with Bricha boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were coming from Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Bricha?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bricha? It's Aliyah Bet. Bricha, that means it's a brigade of illegal army, which later on became a regular Israeli army. And those boys were transporting the people illegally. By boat, by climbing the mountains via Switzerland, to get them to Palestine. And so that was very satisfying for me to know that I have to have people already clean. It consisted that-- being a nurse in DP Enns consisted of delousing people, Russians and Poles, and getting them ready-- especially the fit ones-- to go on a boat to Palestine. After two years of doing it, they-- the English started to drown the boats. So again I felt, there we go. I thought there never will be war. No human being will be doing any injustice to another human being. But that wasn't so. I didn't-- I didn't have to wait very long. The boats were being drowned. So the Bricha, via-- UNRRA, Joint, I don't know which organization. Somehow it came to me that we're not to send any more people. Not to prepare any more people, because the boys cannot pick them up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we just sat there and waited. And I thought, well, it's no point. That's no good to me. I applied to several countries to go overseas, to go somewhere to start normal life. By that time, there was no way of going back into the Russian zone to be with my sister and my mother. They also planned to go over to America, or somewhere, into the world, to start living.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and we all decided-- there was a group of us-- that whichever country will answer work first, that we will go. But of course, being in the camp-- DP camp-- and not being able to read German as well, nor there was any news in the German papers of what was going on, all that we have found out afterwards and via just certain people. Nevertheless, England decided to take the DP people. So I went to England, and I was sent to call-- to a place called Preston. And in Preston, I was told that I have to go to a domestic service, to which I declined. I said no, I want to go to school. I want to learn properly English. I want to become a nurse. That was my ambition. Just to become a nurse to do good. To give back to the humanity, to do something for God to save my life. Well, I must do something with that life. And I fought tremendously. But eventually, they agreed to let me go to hospital. I was delighted till I arrived there. It was place called Roose Infirmary, near a place called Barrow-in-Furness. It was place for aged people who could not wash themselves, for people who could not [SIGHS] look after themselves. All I did was washing them, giving them bed pans, cleaning their teeth. And that was this nursing business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at one stage, while I was in England, I have received a letter from my mother. From time to time, I did get sent some letters or post card. And Mother said that she is going to Australia on such-and-such a date. No name of the boat, because she didn't know. They were going via HIAS. She didn't know exactly the time. Nevertheless, I have pooled all the resources, and all my friends, because group of us went to England. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had been sick at that time, and I asked for leave, for holiday. So the doctor gave me two weeks holiday to see my mother. I went to Italy. Look, this trip is a book in itself. I-- not-- I did not know on what name Mother is traveling, because she remarried since then. Where's she staying, what boat she's going on. And I went to look for her in Genoa.

speaker: interviewee
text: So somebody who wanted me to-- a porter, who wanted my luggage to take to his hotel, that I would be the client, in that he thought I was a tourist. I told him my story in German. He understood very well. Till 2:00 in the morning we were looking for my mother. This-- this was out of this world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, he said, look. Go to sleep. Tomorrow morning I'll come pick you up and we'll look for your mother. We went to HIAS. It was closed because it was Friday. My mother was staying in the same hotel where I was asleep. In the morning I come down to the dining room. I came to the dining room, and I saw my mother and my stepfather sitting there. You know, these stories kept happening all the time to me. I said, that was a miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you arrive in Australia?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got married here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married here, yes. I will--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived November, 1953. I got married in August, '54. I had a wonderful husband, very understanding. He knew how to resolve my conflict. He has been very well educated. He really pulled me out of the camp. Unfortunately, he got cancer and he passed away in 1970. But we had a very-- short marriage, but very happy marriage. 17 years only. We have three beautiful children, and I was left with the children. As little as they were, they were extremely helpful. And I say to this day that they brought me up, not the other way around. They went to high school. Yvette and Noemi were 12. Tamara was 14. They went to university. All three have degrees. Some of them have two degrees. Married to nice Jewish husbands. That was very important to me, I am embarrassed to say. I kept saying to them all the time, as long as they are decent, as long as they are nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Down deep, I felt that, you know, you do need-- you can't mix water and oil. And all the time I taught them, and they brought this back to me. You teach us not to be prejudiced, not to be racist, and you telling us we have to marry Jewish boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I didn't tell you have to. I just said that it would be life easier if you did. And that I always did say. But in my heart I was praying that they should marry Jewish boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think helped you to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just bit of luck. A few times I have managed to help myself, like in Auschwitz, going back in to go second time for the selection. Not many people would have done that. They wouldn't think so quickly. Managing to get extra bread in Auschwitz, where we were starving. And to be starved meant to be weak, and if you're weak you don't survive. Always trying to be straight and always-- whenever I was asked, I was always 18. Always 18 years old. Always ready with the answer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think anything specific. Just the fact that I was lucky, very lucky. Hellga Seigal kept giving me her bread when I was very sick and I needed the bread. And not many people had that relationship with somebody who was willing to-- to do that. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: That made a difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sure it did, especially after typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you agree to this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: Truthfully, the persistence of you people. You people, you and the other girls who rang me. And above everything else, my children asked me. Noemi-- Mommy, please do it. We want to know what you went through. And you know, as much as I did tell you, there is a lot I didn't tell. You just can't tell everything. I have come out with lots of horrible things, but there were a lot of other horrible things which I did not come. It did not embed it in my mind to that extent that it's ready now to come out with it. And I think people should know. People should know. Because maybe that'll help not to do it again, not to create that hatred amongst people. You know, sure, you can have your preferences. You may prefer somebody-- short, or long, or with blond hair, or dark hair. But not to that extent that you are willing to kill somebody because they are not of your own persuasion.

speaker: interviewee
text: And unfortunately, it's not like that at all. People are still killing each other in Yugoslavia, for what? What are they going to gain by it? Land? What is it? What is the matter with us? All the time I turn off the radio and television because they only tell you who got killed, or where is a war. Because they want to sell the arms. What would you like to tell your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Precisely that, that they should be decent human beings. Have respect for other people no matter what color, what nationality. They should respect people for what they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for doing this interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. I thank you, Danusha, for persevering, with keeping on asking me, and ringing me. Because if not for you, I wouldn't have done it. I know that you have understanding for what we went through. And you have personal knowledge from your own experience, from your own family. I have no rapport with other girls who were very sweet and rang or came here to ask. I just couldn't bring myself to. It takes a lot from you, of you, of this poor cameraman to listen, and Celine listen to it. But believe me, it took a lot out of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a-- I won't say it's a pleasure. It's not a pleasure. But I feel it needs to be told.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you tell me who is on this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these are my grandparents, my mother-- this lady-- and her sisters and brothers. There were eight of them at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give some names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, everyone. Auntie Amalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schecter. She was married. Her married name was Schecter. Auntie Ester. She was married to a man called Klausner. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? Recha. ?] She was killed before she was married. And that was Uncle Sulek. It's a boy, although he had a dress. Up to three years old, the children did not have a haircut, they did not get boys' clothes. They were in dress. And that's Auntie [? Chanka, ?] and that's Uncle [? Duchu. ?] He lived in Yaroslavl, which perished with the whole family. And this is [? Hela, ?] which went to America later on. They all were-- except for my mother, who lived in Cracow, and my Auntie Amalia, who lived in Lwow, and this one in America-- they were all congregated on the spot in the village where they lived, they were told to dig a grave, and then shot. And people have covered the grave. And that's what we were told after the war. That's how they perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [INAUDIBLE] Could you tell me who this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Ignacy Schermant, and this is my mother, Mina Schermant. This was taken during the war of my mother. She was decorated with the Iron Cross for nursing cholera during the first World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was taken during the first World War?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Of my mother, not of my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your father's photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say after they were married, after the war ended. Because I can see by the clothes that he was already in civilian clothes. And he was, during the war, an officer. That's how they met.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who's on this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother, the lady on the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blima Pleisner, my mother's mother. Then it's my mother sitting here with the two children. My sister, Francizka Schermant, now Heuberger. My brother, Shimon Schermant, held by my father, Ignacy Schermant. And my auntie Amalia Schecter standing at the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is the same family of my mother. My grandparents on my mother's side. Auntie Mala-- Amalia Schecter-- with her husband. And my uncles, Duchu, Tchoyka, and Sulek. That's what we used to call them. And there in the background stands Rishka and Chanka--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Pleisner. That was taken shortly after that previous pictures you have seen. Must have been a little bit later because I can see that my auntie's already married to [? Lonek ?] Schecter and they are much more grown up. I don't remember the dates. I don't know. I did not-- I was not alive yet. I see my grandparents here look much younger to what I remember them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Francizka Heuberger, my brother, Shimon Schermant, who perished in Russia, and myself in the middle. Henryka Shaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken-- oh, you're asking a lot. It must have been in Cracow. I would have been three years old, not more, and my sister would have been six. Or must have been more. She must have been more because there are six years difference between us and four years between my brother, Shimek, and myself. So three and he would be six, and Nyushka would be nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Possible? Yes, she looks nine. I don't really know the dates correctly. I don't want to mislead you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you tell me about this building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This building is the house we were living in before the war-- well before the war-- and born in. It's in Cracow, Podgorze. When the tram lines were being built, the building developed-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what is it called-- cracks. And it had to be pulled down, but that happened about two, three years before we went to ghetto. We lived a little bit further away, number five. This was Wieliczka number one. And we moved further away to a rented apartment, Wieliczka [NON-ENGLISH]. And the next to it is the block where the house stood, with [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: A shrine.

speaker: interviewee
text: With Jesus mine-- minding my property.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a shrine?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is a shrine, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm standing next to it. That tiny little figure. It was I. Went there to see the place and I stood next to that shrine. I was really pleased that there is something being done with that land, that it's not idle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is on this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, my sister, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in ghetto, just prior to my going into camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you tell me something about this stone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This stone I have brought from the spot-- from the place where my father's body was burned, where the ashes are lying. I brought it from Cracow-- from Poland-- myself. And I had the intention of putting on my mother's grave, and then for various reasons decided not to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you tell me about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is wedding-- my wedding photo. My husband, Rudy Shaw, and myself, in Central Synagogue in Australia, in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. --Want to see. Could you tell me who's on these photos?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my three daughters with their husbands and their children. Tamara Pollak, Bernard Pollak, Harvey Pollak, Ellen, and Marisa. Noemi, Jeff Silberbach, and their first child, Ryan Silberbach. And Michaela, who is nine months, is not on that picture because she wasn't born yet. And Yvette Bogatin with her husband, Lenny, Carly, and Adam. Adam, please God, will be bar mitzvah next year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We all have colds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Shaw, would you please introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my oldest daughter, Tamara Pollak. And here is my daughter, Noemi Silberbach. She's one of the twins. The second twin lives in Melbourne, so unfortunately we can't have it here. And here is my granddaughter, Marisa. Ryan Silberbach, and he's smiling. He is smiling ever so much. And my grandson, Harvey, of whom I am also very proud because he reminds me of my brother, Shimek. And this is Ellenko. Her name is Ellen Pollak. These three belong to Tamara, and little Michaela belongs to Noemi. This is the youngest baby, which we don't have on that picture. And I have two more grandchildren in Melbourne. Yvette Bogatin, Lenny Bogatin, they have two children. Adam, the one who will be bar mitzvah next year, please God I should live so long. I need to live for that occasion. And Carly Bogatin, who is eight and a half.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is my family, my pride and joy. And I have-- Tamara has husband who is wonderful, grand, a great guy. He is my oldest son-in-law. Jeff Silberbach, who unfortunately is in Parramatta. He had to go to work. Somebody has to earn a living while we're enjoying having pictures taken. Aren't we? Would you like to say anything about your mother giving her testimony today?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just feel very privileged that Mum was able. We realize how difficult it was for her. And that she's very special to us all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we realize it's a lot to ask of Mum to do this, and we think it's important for us and our grandchildren, and her grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mrs. Shaw, how do you feel, after having told us your story, to have your family around here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now I feel very emotional and totally privileged to have seven grandchildren, to have three wonderful daughters who have brought me up in the difficult times when my husband passed away. It was very hard. It was not easy at all. We were not left financially secure, but we managed. I was grateful that my children received education, tertiary education. All three. One is a teacher, one is a social worker, one is economist. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't know how to economize, but a master of economics she has. But I do the economizing. I do the keeping the purse. And Ryan will do it after, won't you, Ryechko? Yes. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you, Danusha. I thank you. I think you are marvelous, and I'm grateful that you did it with me. Because you went through it. It was like going through the camp all over again. Certainly at certain moments. But you helped me through it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how it is. And I've got lovely things to look forward to. [INAUDIBLE] They're all in Moriah College, except Ryan and Michaela. And the two in Melbourne are also in Jewish school, in Bialik School. So I feel very privileged that I have instilled in them the love for Judaism. So I know why I suffered, why did all that was worthwhile going through and surviving.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's hope that the Jewish, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not only religion, but the way of life will survive and will be passed on to the grandchildren and to their children and grandchildren. I hope they will remember all that. When they are older, I will show them the tape. Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
